assessor_1_raw:
ASSESSMENT 1:
Two genuine deployment concerns: (1) Bottom-quintile stores show 19.4% MAPE — 2.3x the overall model and above any reasonable threshold for fully autonomous perishable ordering. These stores account for fewer than 3% of chain sales, but per-store operational impact from perishable waste and stockouts is material. (2) No human override capability is architecturally risky for perishable goods. Exception handling for demand spikes, supply anomalies, and model errors is necessary. Full-autonomy, no-override deployment to all 400 stores is not supportable as proposed. Tiered deployment and minimum exception process required. Verdict: EMPIRICAL_TEST_AGREED (the question of what MAPE threshold is acceptable for perishable autonomous ordering is empirically contestable).
assessor_2_raw:
ASSESSMENT 2:
19.4% MAPE for bottom-quintile stores is problematic for perishable goods. These stores are 3% of sales but real stores where errors matter operationally. No override capability is an architectural gap — there is no recovery mechanism for model errors in time-sensitive perishable orders. The full-autonomy, no-override, all-stores proposal requires modification. A tiered deployment based on model performance quintile, with exception handling, would address both concerns. Verdict: EMPIRICAL_TEST_AGREED.
assessor_3_raw:
ASSESSMENT 3:
Low-volume store performance gap (19.4% vs. 8.3% overall) is a genuine concern for perishable autonomous ordering. The no-override design creates systemic risk for high-demand exceptions. Both concerns are valid. The deployment is supportable for high-volume stores under full autonomy; modifications are needed for low-volume stores and exception handling. Verdict: EMPIRICAL_TEST_AGREED.
adjudication_raw: All three assessors agree on the two issues. When synthesizing: this case involves a contested empirical claim — specifically, what level of MAPE constitutes acceptable accuracy for fully autonomous perishable ordering at low-volume stores. The empirical test specification below addresses this.

empirical_test.measure: Perishable waste rate and stockout frequency in bottom-quintile stores under ML-driven autonomous ordering vs. rule-based system, over a 60-day pilot period; comparison of order accuracy (MAPE realized vs. holdout MAPE) in production conditions
empirical_test.success_criterion: Waste rate and stockout frequency in bottom-quintile stores not materially worse than in top-quintile stores or vs. rule-based baseline; realized MAPE in production within 3 percentage points of holdout MAPE (19.4%)
empirical_test.failure_criterion: Waste rate or stockout frequency in bottom-quintile stores substantially elevated vs. baseline or top-quintile stores; realized production MAPE > 25% in bottom-quintile stores
